title: Ambiguous source-rendered fields resolve via clinical reasoning with auto-flag
description: When a level-2 source (e.g. Case Book PDF) renders a question in a way that is ambiguous between two valid interpretations (e.g. 'o' symbols for a checklist could mean radio-button single-select OR checkbox multi-select), the skill resolves the ambiguity using clinical reasoning and emits a review_flags.choice_list_review entry. DETECTION: ambiguity flagged when (a) a list of options appears in the source with markers (o, □, (_)) visually consistent with multiple input types, AND (b) the source does not specify input type via accompanying instruction (e.g. 'select all that apply' vs 'select one'). RESOLUTION PRINCIPLE: the skill defaults to the interpretation that PRESERVES MORE DATA — for check-style lists where multiple values can plausibly co-occur (implanted devices, comorbidities, co-medications), default to select_multiple; for mutually-exclusive categories (NYHA class, AFL type), default to select_one; when clinical reasoning is unclear, default to select_multiple (lossless). EVERY AMBIGUITY-RESOLVED FIELD is recorded with form, field, case_book_section, issue description, skill_decision rationale, and action_required='Verify with sponsor.' UNIVERSAL SCOPE per §0.
rationale: Source PDFs frequently use generic checkbox-like symbols that don't unambiguously specify whether the question is single-select or multi-select. Defaulting to select_multiple where ambiguity exists preserves more information than defaulting to select_one: a sponsor-clarified single-select can be tightened from multi-select after the fact, but a sponsor-clarified multi-select cannot be expanded from single-select without losing data already entered. Clinical-domain reasoning (devices and comorbidities are typically plural; NYHA class is singular) provides a useful prior; flagging every decision for sponsor verification preserves auditability.

history.note: Imported from conventions.md §24 during Phase B.1d. Advisory because the rule describes a resolution principle the skill applies during protocol-analysis source interpretation, not a mechanical check the engine can fire. The detection ('source markers ambiguous between radio and checkbox') requires PDF-vision capability the engine doesn't have; the resolution ('default to lossless multi-select') requires clinical-domain judgment Claude provides during build. Renders into Claude's prompt context as guidance for ambiguity resolution. The auto-flagging requirement (every resolution recorded in review_flags.choice_list_review) is enforced by the skill's PDF-to-spec extraction step, not by the conventions engine.
